segments.s503-g41: به here means 'towards/into' and marks the goal/result of the action. چنگال (claw/talon) is the goal noun placed before the verb. می‌کرد is the imperfective/past-habitual auxiliary 'would do / used to do' (classical literary Past continuous) and takes the following object منقار تیز ('sharp beak'), which is the thing being turned into a talon.
segments.s503-g42: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ایمن شد is a simple past/resultative clause 'became safe' (شد = 'became'). از + noun gives the source ('from'); گردش رستخیز is a prepositional phrase 'the movement/turning of the rüştaxīz' (rüştaxīz is an archaic/poetic word meaning an uprising/resurrection).
segments.s503-g43: به قیصر بفرمود = 'he said/commanded to the Caesar'; به is the dative 'to' before the addressee; بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause ('so that / in order that'), and بی‌گروه ('without company') modifies the verb in that subordinate clause.
segments.s503-g44: پیاده شود is a subjunctive/purpose construction: پیاده 'on foot; to dismount' plus شود (here functioning as the subordinate/modal auxiliary 'should/be to') — together 'should go on foot / dismount'. بر سر is a locative 'on the summit/top of', and تیغ کوه is a poetic image 'the mountain's blade/razor' (the ridge or sharp crest).
segments.s503-g45: ببیند is a subjunctive/optative form ('that he should see' / 'to see'); که introduces the content clause. تا here has the sense 'even / as far as' emphasizing the summit; بر سر کوه چیست = 'what is upon the mountain's summit' with چیست as the copula 'is'.
segments.s503-g46: کزو is a relative/prepositional compound 'from/of which' introducing the result clause. شادمان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker and šādmān is 'the rejoicing/joyful (people)'. بباید is the modal 'must/ought to' and گریست is the verb 'to weep / to lament' — together 'one must weep' or 'would have to weep'. The whole clause means 'which would make even the joyful have to weep.'
segments.s503-g25: ورا is the indirect object 'for him/it' placed before the verb in archaic word order. شاد modifies مردم ('the people joyful'). نخواند is negated خواند (here 'make/sing/call someone joyful'); with the particle همی it carries a habitual/continuous or past-regular sense: 'would not cause (them) to be joyful.'
segments.s503-g26: وگر is the conditional 'even if / but if'. جان و دل is a paired object 'life and heart' (i.e. one's very life). برفشاند (3rd singular) with همی gives a counterfactual/habitual sense 'were to scatter/pour out'; here it means 'to give up/shed' life and heart for someone.
segments.s503-g27: به خاک آمد is a verbal phrase 'came to the earth / fell to the ground' (به + noun + آمد). از بر شده is a participial clause 'having been separated/torn from (it)' where از indicates separation and بر = 'on/upon'; شده is the perfect/passive participle. چوب عمود is a noun phrase 'the wooden shaft/staff of the column.'
segments.s503-g28: تهی ماند is 'remained empty' (تهی predicate, ماند past of ماندن). زان = 'from that / because of that', linking back to the previous clause. مرغ رنگین عمود is a genitive/grouped phrase 'the colorful bird's perch/column' (i.e. the perch used by the bright bird).
segments.s503-g29: بپرسید is a past/preterite third-person form 'he asked'. دانایی و راستی are the objects/topics of the question — 'knowledge (wisdom) and truthfulness/integrity' — joined by و.
segments.s503-g30: فزونست is an older form meaning 'is greater / increases' (فزون + است). اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. کمی و کاستی are two near-synonymous nouns 'a little / smallness' and 'deficiency/shortcoming' — together 'if there is some lack/defect'.
segments.s503-g31: چنین داد پاسخ is 'thus gave an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'); که introduces the reported content ('that'). دانش‌پژوه is the subject of the reported clause meaning 'the scholar/student/scholarly man.'
segments.s503-g32: همی is the habitual/continuous particle. سرفرازد (3rd singular) means 'is raised/exalted/stands proud'. ز هر دو گروه uses ز in a comparative/ablative sense 'above/of/from' and هر دو گروه 'both groups' — together 'exalted above both groups.'
segments.s503-g1: سکندر is the subject (proper name). سوی روشنایی is a prepositional phrase meaning "toward the light" (سوی + noun), placed after the name for poetic word order. رسید is a past tense verb meaning "arrived/reached," completing the clause (S + PP + V, typical classical flexibility).
segments.s503-g2: یکی is an indefinite pronoun functioning as the subject here ("someone / a certain one"). بر شد is a two-part verbal expression meaning "rose up" or "got up," with بر acting as an 'up' particle and شد the past tense of شدن. کوه رخشنده دید is a following clause where دید (past of دیدن) takes کوه رخشنده as its object: "saw a shining mountain."
segments.s503-g3: زده is a past participle derived from زدن used here in the sense 'set up/erected' and modifies عمود (the pillar). بر سر کوه خارا is a prepositional phrase 'upon the summit of the hard/flinty mountain' placed before the head noun (poetic inversion), so the literal ordering is 'set/erected upon the head (summit) of the flinty mountain a pillar.'
segments.s503-g4: سرش is سر + enclitic ش meaning 'its top' (the possessive refers back to the pillar). تا به ابر اندر is a spatial clause: تا = 'as far as / up to,' به ... اندر = 'into / within the cloud' (اندر is an older/poetic preposition for 'in'). از چوب عود is a material phrase 'of / from the wood of oud (agarwood),' with از marking source/material.
segments.s503-g5: بر هر عمودی is a prepositional phrase 'on every upright/pillar' (بر + هر + عمودی). کنامی بزرگ follows as the noun phrase meaning a 'great konam' — کنام is an older/poetic noun (lit. 'bosom/seat' or side/crest) used here for a platform/seat; بزرگ is the adjective 'great.' The phrase order (prep + noun + modifier) is poetic but straightforward.
segments.s503-g6: نشسته is a perfect-participial/relative form meaning 'having sat' or simply 'sat.' برو in this line is best read as a poetic contraction of بر او ("on it") rather than the verb 'go' (so it functions as a locative 'on it'). سبز مرغی سترگ is a noun phrase 'a green bird (مرغی) with wide gaze/large eyes (سترگ),' where the adjectives and qualifiers follow in the compact, descriptive classical style.
segments.s503-g7: به آواز رومی literally 'in the voice/tone of Rûm' — here به + (noun) has the adverbial sense 'in (the language/voice of)'. آواز = 'voice/tone,' رومی = 'Rûmi / Byzantine' (i.e., the Rûm language). سخن راندند is an idiomatic compound: سخن (speech) + راندند (lit. 'drove') meaning 'they uttered/spoke.'
segments.s503-g8: جهاندار is the noun 'sovereign / world-ruler' and پیروز is the epithet 'victorious'; را is the definite object marker indicating that جهاندار پیروز is the direct object. خواندند here means 'they called / summoned' (not merely 'read'), completing the sense: 'they called the victorious sovereign.'
segments.s503-g33: به سوی عمود آمد از تیره خاک is arranged with the directional phrase (به سوی + noun) before the verb: 'آمد' is the past tense 'came'. 'از تیره خاک' is an ablative phrase 'from the dark earth/dust' giving the origin. This is a compact, poetic word order (direction → verb → source).
segments.s503-g34: به منقار چنگالها کرد پاک uses به + instrument to indicate means ('with/by the beak'), then the object 'چنگالها' (the talons), followed by the light verb 'کرد' ('made/did') and the adjective 'پاک' ('clean'). The verb phrase thus literally says 'made (them) clean with the beak' = 'cleaned its talons with its beak.'
segments.s503-g35: ز قیصر بپرسید یزدان‌پرست puts the preposition ز ('from/of') before 'قیصر' to mean 'asked of Caesar'; 'بپرسید' is a past-tense third-person form 'asked' and the subject 'یزدان‌پرست' (the pious man) follows the verb, a common inversion in poetry.
segments.s503-g36: به شهر تو بر کوه دارد نشست places the location phrase 'به شهر تو' ('at/for your town') before 'بر کوه' ('on the mountain'); 'دارد' is present/persistent 'has' (possesses / auxiliary) and 'نشست' is used as 'sitting/seat' or 'has seated himself' (verb sense 'sit/settle'). Together they ask whether someone 'has a seat/is seated on the mountain above your town.'
segments.s503-g37: بدو گفت چون مرد شد پاک‌رای begins with the indirect-object adverb 'بدو' ('to him'), then the verb 'گفت' ('said'). 'چون' here introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when' / 'as'), followed by 'مرد شد' ('a man became') and the compound adjective 'پاک‌رای' ('of pure/good reputation'). The clause functions as the protasis (when X happens) of the following result clause.
segments.s503-g38: بیابد پرستنده بر کوه جای is the apodosis: 'بیابد' (third-person present/subjunctive/optative form 'finds/will find') is the verb, 'پرستنده' is the subject 'the worshipper', and 'بر کوه جای' is the locative object 'a place on the mountain.' The construction says that, when the condition holds, the worshipper will find a place on the mountain.
segments.s503-g39: ازان چوب جوینده شد بر کنام starts with the ablative 'ازان' ('from that'), then 'چوب' ('wood') as source; 'جوینده شد' ('became/appeared as a seeker') uses 'شد' as the past of 'become/appear'. 'بر کنام' is a locative phrase 'on the bosom/lap' giving where he appeared or was placed. The overall compression is typical of epic poetry.
segments.s503-g40: جهانجوی روشن‌دل و شادکام is an appositive noun phrase describing that seeker: 'جهانجوی' (world-seeker/ambitious one) followed by two adjectives 'روشن‌دل' (clear‑hearted) and 'شادکام' (fortunate/joyful), joined by 'و'. It gives characteristic epithets in the usual epic style.
segments.s503-g17: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed clause 'thus gave answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = past 3rd singular 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). کزین begins the subordinate clause: کزین = ک + از + این (poetic contraction) meaning 'from/among these' or 'that from this', followed by هر دو 'both' (هر = 'each/every' used here in the idiom 'هر دو' = 'both'), and هست is the copula 'is' (present/past-state). Word order is typical classical Persian: main clause ('thus gave answer') + relative clause introduced by کزین giving the predicate 'there is X between these two.'
segments.s503-g18: زنا is the predicative noun 'adultery' completing 'هست' from the previous segment ('there is adultery'). و simply connects the next clause. برین is the prepositional phrase بر + این (archaic spelling ین) = 'on/upon this', where ین is an orthographic variant of این; گونه modifies جای as 'this kind of'. جای نشست = 'sat down on the place/seat' (نشست = past 'sat').
segments.s503-g19: چو functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (3rd person), followed by the object پاسخ 'the reply'. فروتر is an adverbial comparative 'lower' (i.e., 'farther down' or 'lower on the seat'), and نشست is the past 'sat' (he sat down). The sequence gives a single action: 'when he heard the answer, he sat down lower.'
segments.s503-g20: درو is a contraction meaning 'at it / upon it / concerning it' (در + او → درِ او → درو) and links back to the prior answer. خیره شد is a light-verb construction 'became astonished/stared' (خیره = 'staring/astonished', شد = 'became'). مرد یزدان‌پرست is the subject 'the pious/God-worshipping man' placed after the verb phrase in inverted classical order.
segments.s503-g21: بپرسید is the past narrative 'he asked'. کاندر is a fused form meaning 'in which / wherein' (کان + در), followed by جهان 'the world'. بانگ رود is a relative clause 'the cry/sound that runs/goes' — here بانگ = 'sound/cry' and رود functions as a verbal form 'goes / runs through' (the provided senses for رود do not include the verbal sense, so the lexical sense list doesn't match the verb use).
segments.s503-g22: شنیدی is the interrogative/past form 'have you heard?' (used as the content of 'he asked'). و simply links the next nouns. آوای = 'the voice/melody of' and مست is adjectival 'intoxicated' or 'intoxicating' (figurative here, modifying آوا). و سرود = 'and the song' (سرود = noun 'song/ode'). The series lists things being heard.
segments.s503-g23: چنین داد پاسخ = 'he gave such an answer'. که introduces the content clause ('that'). هر کو is the classical correlative 'whoever' (هر = 'each/every', کو is the archaic relative 'who/which'); ز دهر = 'from the age' i.e. 'in this life' or 'in the world' (ز = poetic 'from/of', دهر = 'time/age'). Note: کو here is the archaic relative particle 'who', which is not among the provided senses for کو and so is marked separately.
segments.s503-g24: ز شادی = 'from/for joy' (ز = 'from/of', شادی = 'joy/celebration'). همی before the verb is a classical continuous/imperfective marker often used by Ferdowsi ('still/continually' or forming habitual aspect). برنگیرند is a 3rd-person plural negative-present/habitual meaning 'they do not take' or 'do not seize' (here idiomatically 'refrain from taking/accepting joy'); بهر = 'for / for the sake of / to the advantage of'. The clause expresses 'refrain from joy for some advantage.'
segments.s503-g9: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. آواز بشنید is a simple past clause: بشنید is 3rd‑person past 'he heard' with آواز 'the call/voice' as object. قیصر is the subject (emperor/Caesar). برفت is an archaic past verb 'he went forward/advanced' (poetic past), placed after the subject in inverted poetic order to mean 'he went forward.'
segments.s503-g10: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near to' (به + نزدیک). مرغان is the plural noun 'the birds' and serves as the subject of خرامید. خرامید is a past tense verb 'strode/strutted proudly' (poetic 3rd‑person past). تفت here is an isolated particle/onomatopoeic or obscure word; its precise meaning is unclear in the supplied senses, so it is left as an indecipherable exclamation or epithet attached to the clause.
segments.s503-g11: بدو is the clitic/prepositional pronoun 'to him' (i.e. 'said to him'). مرغ is the subject 'the bird'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used as a verb; the dictionary senses given are not the verbal sense so the proper verbal sense is unlisted). ای is the vocative particle 'O', دلارای is a vocative adjective 'beloved/charming', and رنج is a noun 'sorrow/suffering' modifying or qualifying the addressee ('O beloved in sorrow').
segments.s503-g12: چه جویی همی is an archaic present interrogative: چه 'what', جویی is 2nd‑person present 'you seek' (what do you seek), and همی is the classical present/continuous particle adding immediacy ('do you still/now seek'). زین is a contracted form of 'از این' ('from/of this') and heads the following noun phrase; سرای is 'house/abode' (here used as a locative), and سپنج is an obscure or specific noun/name attached to the house (its exact meaning is unclear in the supplied senses). The whole phrase asks 'what do you seek from this house (سپنج)?'.
segments.s503-g13: اگر is the conditional 'if'. سر برآری is a classical conditional clause meaning 'you lift/raise your head' — برآری here is an archaic/subjunctive/present form 'you raise' (used after اگر). به چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'to the high/lofty sky' (چرخ = sky/heaven, بلند = lofty).
segments.s503-g14: همان functions as a resumptive/emphatic particle here ('then' / 'indeed' / 'that [thing]'), and بازگردی is 2nd‑person present/future 'you will return' (poetic present used as result of the conditional). ازو = 'from it/from there', and مستمند is a predicate adjective 'needy/poor/afflicted' describing the subject on return. Poetic word order places the predicate after the prepositional phrase.
segments.s503-g15: کنون 'now' opens the rhetorical question. کامدی is an archaic verbal form (here roughly 'have you come' / 'have you ever come/seen'); هیچ functions as 'ever' (intensifier in questions), دیدی is the 2nd‑person past/present 'have you seen' (used in the interrogative), and زنا is the noun 'adultery'. Together this yields the rhetorical 'Have you ever seen any adultery now?'
segments.s503-g16: وگر is 'but if / or if'. کرده is the past participle or perfect 'made' (here functioning with the following phrase), از is the preposition 'from/of', خشت پخته is a noun phrase 'baked brick' (خشت 'brick', پخته 'baked'), and بنا is 'building/structure'. The sequence describes an alternative: 'or if it has been made as a building of baked brick.' Word order is poetic: the participle precedes the material phrase.

sentences.s503-s21: He turned his sharp beak into a talon when he was safe from the tumult of the uprising.
sentences.s503-s22: He ordered the Caesar that, without escort, he should dismount on the razor-like crest of the mountain.
sentences.s503-s23: —to see what was on the mountain's summit that would force even the joyful to weep.
sentences.s503-s13: He would not make the people rejoice for him, even if he were to pour out his life and heart.
sentences.s503-s14: The wooden shaft of the column, having been torn off, fell to the ground; the bright bird's perch remained empty.
sentences.s503-s15: He asked about knowledge and truthfulness: do they increase when there is a little lack?
sentences.s503-s16: Thus he answered that the scholar is exalted above both groups.
sentences.s503-s1: Alexander came toward the light; someone rose up and saw a shining mountain.
sentences.s503-s2: On the summit of the flinty mountain a pillar was set up; its top reached into the cloud and was of agarwood.
sentences.s503-s3: On each upright (pillar) a great konam (seat/crest) sat; upon it sat a large green bird with wide gaze.
sentences.s503-s4: They spoke in the Rûmi tongue; they called the victorious sovereign.
sentences.s503-s17: It came toward the column from the dark earth; it cleaned its talons with its beak.
sentences.s503-s18: The God-worshipper asked Caesar whether he had taken a seat on the mountain above your town.
sentences.s503-s19: He said to him: when a man becomes of good repute, the worshipper will find a place on the mountain.
sentences.s503-s20: From that wood a seeker came into the lap: a world-seeker, clear‑hearted and fortunate.
sentences.s503-s9: He answered thus: that between the two there is adultery, and upon this sort of seat he sat down.
sentences.s503-s10: When he heard the reply he sat lower; the pious man stood amazed at it.
sentences.s503-s11: He asked, 'Have you heard the sound that goes through the world, and the intoxicating melody and song?'
sentences.s503-s12: He answered thus: that whoever, in this life, refrains from joy for some advantage...
sentences.s503-s5: When the Caesar heard the call he went forward and drew near; the birds strutted proudly.
sentences.s503-s6: To him the bird said, 'O beloved in suffering, what do you seek from this house (Sepanj)?'
sentences.s503-s7: If you lift your head to the high sky, you will return from it needy.
sentences.s503-s8: Now have you ever seen any adultery? — or if it has been made, [is it] a building of baked brick?
